name: Fintech & Digital Banking official sources
notes: Fintech holds some of the site's most price-perishable public numbers: mid-market FX rates, remittance provider fees and margins, payment-gateway and POS fees, digital-lending APRs, mobile-money charges, deposit and T-bill benchmark rates. FX and provider pricing move faster than any review cadence.

Unlike energy/telecom, fintech has no single country dataset file. Each tool embeds its own data (per-country option attributes or a provider table), and mid-market FX is now pulled live. This ledger therefore binds sources by CLASS OF CLAIM, not by a single dataset row.

Mid-market FX is sourced: remittance-compare (and b2b-payment for spread reference) now read live USD-based rates from the shared forex feed (/api/forex?base=USD, backed by data/forex/latest.json). A corridor rate is rates[dest]/rates[send]; when a currency is missing the tool falls back to a March 2026 benchmark snapshot rather than defaulting to zero.

Deposit, T-bill, bond and money-market BENCHMARK rates are anchored to central-bank policy rates and treasury auctions. The central bank is authoritative for the policy rate and the T-bill/bond auction yield; specific commercial-bank deposit offers shown in the tools are indicative and editable by the user.

Provider PRICING is commercial by design and recorded as unsourced: remittance fees/margins, payment-gateway and POS fees, digital-lending and microfinance APRs, BNPL fees, mobile-money charges, and SACCO dividend rates are offers, not regulated rates. They are recorded in gaps.unsourcedClaims exactly as energy records solar-kit prices.

Never update a benchmark rate, fee, APR or FX figure because a URL's content hash changed. Read the gazetted rate notice, auction result or published tariff first, exactly as .claude/rules/government.md and .claude/rules/energy.md require.

The bug class to watch, per .claude/rules/telecom.md and .claude/rules/energy.md: `country.data.field || 0` turns missing data into free, which then wins a cost comparison. A July 2026 audit found NO instance of this in the 32 fintech tools — every per-country/per-provider lookup either reads a complete data-* attribute into an editable input or is guarded by an explicit `if (!x) { alert; return }`. remittance-compare guards a missing corridor at index.html and must keep doing so. Preserve that; never let a future market or provider default a fee/rate/FX to zero.

fxFeed.note: Shared live FX source of record for the whole site. remittance-compare reads it on load and computes corridor mid-rates as rates[dest]/rates[send], falling back to a March 2026 snapshot per corridor. Do not hardcode a competing FX table in a fintech tool.

sources.title: Shared live FX source — /api/forex?base=USD
sources.notes: Live USD-based mid-market rates. This is an aggregator, acceptable for mid-market FX because it is a neutral reference (not a provider quote). A provider's own live quote always outranks it for a specific transfer. Backed by data/forex/latest.json as offline fallback.

sources.title: CBN — policy rate & NTB auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the MPR and Nigerian Treasury Bill auction results. Read the published auction result before changing a benchmark yield; commercial-bank deposit offers remain indicative and user-editable.

sources.title: CBK — CBR & Treasury auctions
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Central Bank Rate and the 91/182/364-day Treasury bill auction. Read the auction result before changing a benchmark yield.

sources.title: SARB — repo rate
sources.notes: Authoritative for the repo rate and treasury issuance. Commercial deposit rates remain indicative.

sources.title: BoG — policy rate & T-bill auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Monetary Policy Rate and weekly T-bill auction. Read the auction result before changing a benchmark yield.

sources.title: CBE — policy rate & T-bill auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the overnight rates and Egyptian T-bill auctions.

sources.title: BoT — policy rate & T-bill auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Tanzanian policy rate and treasury auctions.

sources.title: BoU — CBR & T-bill auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Central Bank Rate and Ugandan treasury auctions.

sources.title: BNR — policy rate
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Rwandan central bank rate and treasury auctions.

sources.title: Bank Al-Maghrib — key rate
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Moroccan key rate and treasury auctions.

sources.title: NBE — policy rate
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Ethiopian national bank rate. Read the published notice before changing a benchmark.

sources.title: BoZ — policy rate & T-bill auction
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Zambian policy rate and treasury auctions.

sources.title: Bank of Botswana — MoPR
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Monetary Policy Rate of Botswana.

sources.title: Bank of Namibia — repo rate
sources.notes: Authoritative for the Namibian repo rate.

sources.authority: BCEAO (West African Economic and Monetary Union)
sources.title: BCEAO — WAEMU policy rate (XOF)
sources.notes: Authoritative for the XOF zone (Senegal, Cote d'Ivoire, and the other WAEMU members) policy rate and regional treasury auctions.

sources.authority: BEAC (Central African Economic and Monetary Community)
sources.title: BEAC — CEMAC policy rate (XAF)
sources.notes: Authoritative for the XAF zone (Cameroon and the other CEMAC members) policy rate and regional treasury auctions.

regulatorsWithoutUrl.why: Angola's deposit benchmark is surfaced by fixed-deposit but the BNA URL is not bound here pending verification. Treat the shown rate as planning-grade.
Malawi's deposit benchmark is surfaced by fixed-deposit but the RBM URL is not bound here pending verification. Treat the shown rate as planning-grade.
Mozambique's deposit benchmark is surfaced by fixed-deposit but the central-bank URL is not bound here pending verification. Treat the shown rate as planning-grade.
Zimbabwe's deposit tool shows a USD figure; the RBZ URL is not bound here pending verification, and the multi-currency regime makes any single benchmark fragile.

unsourcedClaims.why: Remittance provider transfer fees and FX margins (Wise, WorldRemit, Remitly, LemFi, Sendwave, Afriex, Western Union, MoneyGram, Chipper) are commercial offers from a March 2026 pricing snapshot, not regulated rates. The recipient's live quote is the only authority for a specific transfer.

Payment-gateway pricing (Paystack, Flutterwave, Stripe, Chipper, Interswitch, Peach, DPO, Pesapal) is commercial and changes with negotiated merchant terms. The 0% bank-transfer rows are the provider's advertised structure, not a claim that settlement is free of all cost.
POS merchant discount rates, terminal rental and caps are commercial acquirer offers that vary by merchant category and volume.

Digital-lending and microfinance APRs (Branch, Tala, FairMoney, Carbon, Zenka, OKash, Jumo, LAPO, FINCA) are commercial and change frequently; some are quoted as fee-per-cycle rather than APR. Verify the lender's own disclosure before borrowing.
Mobile-money send/withdraw tariffs (M-Pesa, MTN MoMo, Airtel Money) are operator tariffs that change by band and market; the tools take them as user input rather than asserting a rate.
SACCO / cooperative dividend and thrift returns are member-society declarations, not regulated rates. Some markets legitimately declare 0% in a given year.

Cross-border B2B provider FX spreads (SWIFT correspondent, Wise Business, Payoneer, Flutterwave) are modelled as indicative percentage spreads, not a live per-provider quote. Confirm the provider's live rate before funding.
